coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient heterostructures of C3N4 and o-GQDs with enrichment of specific oxygen-containing groups for photocatalytic applications
coredata.dc:creator: Nie, Youliang
Bao, Rui
Yi, Jianhong
Tao, Jingmei
Liu, Peng
Ma, Ruofei
Luo, Haichuan
Ma, Danyang
coredata.dc:description:
Photocatalytic effectiveness of the graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/carbon nitride (C3N4) heterostructures is constrained even after tedious pre-modification due to the poor binding issue. In this work, GQDs (enriched with specific oxygen-containing groups (o-GQDs)) were first prepared by simple oxidative cleavage of graphene oxide in nitric acid, and they were then hydrothermally treated with C3N4 (obtained by pyrolysis of melamine without any further modification) to construct o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures. The photocatalytic degradation of the o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures to methyl orange showed outstanding activity, and the photocatalytic performance was increased by 223 times, 18 times, and 14 times to pure C3N4, H-C3N4 and GO/C3N4, respectively. This huge improvement is mainly owing to the perfect combination of o-GQDs and C3N4, which significantly enhances the visible light absorption, the separation rate of photogenerated electrons and holes, and eventually accelerates the charge transfer rate during redox. This enables o-GQDs/C3N4 to generate more superoxide radicals (·O
2
- ) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) under visible light than that of C3N4 and exhibit efficient photocatalytic performance. Our results show that as the unique metal-free photocatalyst, the perfectly combined o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures provides an efficient and environmentally friendly strategy for the removal of water pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: O-GQDs
GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures
Perfect combination
Photocatalytic performance
Radicals

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM image of GO. b) TEM image of o-GQDs (inset: the particle size distribution). c) HR-TEM image of the o-GQDs. d) XRD patterns of GO and o-GQDs. e) UV–vis absorption spectra of o-GQDs (Inset: from left to right, aqueous dispersion of o-GQDs under visible light, Tindal effect under red laser pointer irradiation, and luminescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation). f) Fluorescence spectra of the o-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths.

a) FTIR spectra. b) Raman spectra. c) XPS survey spectra of the GO and o-GQDs. d-e) C1s and O1s High-resolution XPS spectra of GO and o-GQDs, respectively. f) Zeta potential of GO and o-GQDs dispersed in deionized water.
SEM image of a) C3N4, b) H-C3N4, c) GO/C3N4, d) o-GQDs/C3N4.TEM image of e) C3N4, f) GO/C3N4, g) o-GQDs/C3N4. h) HR-TEM image of the o-GQDs/C3N4.
a) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms of C3N4, H-C3N4, GO/C3N4, and o-GQDs/C3N4. b) Pore size distributions of C3N4, H-C3N4, GO/C3N4, and o-GQDs/C3N4.

a) XRD patterns of the C3N4, H-C3N4, GO/C3N4, and o-GQDs/C3N4. b) the enlarged view of (002) peaks of the C3N4, H-C3N4, GO/C3N4, and o-GQDs/C3N4. c) FTIR spectra of C3N4, H-C3N4, GO/C3N4, and o-GQDs/C3N4, respectively. d-f) C1s, N1s, and O1s high-resolution XPS spectra of C3N4 and o-GQDs/C3N4, respectively.

a) MO degradation performance of the C3N4, H-C3N4, GO/C3N4, and o-GQDs/C3N4 under visible light irradiation. b) linear fittings of pseudo-first-order kinetics model. c) cycling runs photodegradation of MO in the presence of o-GQDs/C3N4 under visible light irradiation. d) XRD patterns of o-GQDs/C3N4 before and after cyclic degradation of MO.

a) UV–vis diffuse-reflectance spectra, b) Tauc plots of C3N4, H-C3N4, GO/C3N4, and o-GQDs/C3N4. c) VB XPS spectrum of o-GQDs. d) VB XPS spectrum of C3N4 and o-GQDs/C3N4. e) Band gap structure of C3N4, o-GQDs, and o-GQDs/C3N4. f) Photoluminescence spectra, g) Transient photocurrent response, h) Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of C3N4, H-C3N4, GO/C3N4, and o-GQDs/C3N4.

ESR spectra of C3N4 and o-GQDs/C3N4 in a) methanol dispersion for DMPO-•O
2
- . b) aqueous dispersion for DMPO-•OH under visible light irradiation. c) and d) Degradation efficiency of MO by GQDs/C3N4 with different scavengers under visible light irradiation.
The mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic performance of the o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient heterostructures of C3N4 and o-GQDs with enrichment of specific oxygen-containing groups for photocatalytic applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials of Yunnan Province, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials of Yunnan Province, Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Photocatalytic effectiveness of the graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/carbon nitride (C3N4) heterostructures is constrained even after tedious pre-modification due to the poor binding issue. In this work, GQDs (enriched with specific oxygen-containing groups (o-GQDs)) were first prepared by simple oxidative cleavage of graphene oxide in nitric acid, and they were then hydrothermally treated with C3N4 (obtained by pyrolysis of melamine without any further modification) to construct o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures. The photocatalytic degradation of the o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures to methyl orange showed outstanding activity, and the photocatalytic performance was increased by 223 times, 18 times, and 14 times to pure C3N4, H-C3N4 and GO/C3N4, respectively. This huge improvement is mainly owing to the perfect combination of o-GQDs and C3N4, which significantly enhances the visible light absorption, the separation rate of photogenerated electrons and holes, and eventually accelerates the charge transfer rate during redox. This enables o-GQDs/C3N4 to generate more superoxide radicals (·O
2
- ) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) under visible light than that of C3N4 and exhibit efficient photocatalytic performance. Our results show that as the unique metal-free photocatalyst, the perfectly combined o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures provides an efficient and environmentally friendly strategy for the removal of water pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy and environment-related problems are currently severely burdened as a result of the discharge of industrial effluent, which has greatly polluted water resources [1,2]. Photocatalytic degradation of contaminants is one of the best strategies to efficiently deal with water contamination [3]. However, the most widely used photocatalytic systems now contain metal atoms, which restrict further development due to their high cost and toxicity [4]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a new metal-free photocatalyst. As an emerging star of metal-free photocatalyst, C3N4 has many advantages like low cost, simple preparation, good stability, non-toxicity, and moderate bandgap (~2.70 eV) [5–10]. One of the most significant applications for C3N4 is the catalytic degradation of water pollutants by the visible light component of sunlight. However, the shortcomings of pure C3N4, such as small specific surface area, weak absorption of visible light, and fast recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, limit its practical application [11–13]. The creation of heterostructure photocatalysts, which may boost C3N4 photocatalytic activity by enhancing visible light responsiveness as well as photoacoustic electron-hole pair separation, is a rapid and effective solution to the aforementioned problems [14,15].

Carbon dots (CDs) have been proved to be appropriate materials for building heterostructures to improve the photocatalytic activity of C3N4 because of their remarkable physicochemical and optical properties [16–18]. As a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, CDs exhibit unique up-conversion photoluminescence, high-efficiency broad-spectrum absorption, and excellent photoinduced electron transfer ability [19–22]. For instance, He et al. [23] developed a two-step hydrothermal method to construct a graphene-carbon quantum dots/g-C3N4 nanosheet (GA-CQDs/CNN) ternary photocatalyst, and the degradation efficiency of methyl orange was 7.7 times higher than that of CCN. Liu et al. [14] utilized the template method to synthesize mpg-C3N4 (mesoporous graphitic carbon nitride), and then adopted static adsorption to prepare GQDs/mpg-C3N4 composites for the degradation of rhodamine B. The degradation rate was 42 % higher than that of mpg-C3N4. Zhang et al. [24] synthesized carbon dots (CDs)-modified g-C3N4 photocatalysts using the impregnation-thermal technique. The degradation efficiency of phenol under visible light was increased by 3.7 times.

However, there are still certain issues with building CDs/C3N4 heterostructures: (1) It is challenging to combine CDs and C3N4 perfectly; (2) it is necessary to treat C3N4 with tedious modification; (3) the enhancement of the catalytic activity of CDs/C3N4 heterostructures is less than expected. Therefore, the present study is focused on carbon dot modification.

CDs will vary according to their carbon core, surface states, and properties [25,26]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are small graphene nanosheets with a horizontal size less than 10 nm, which consist of single- or multi-layer graphene sheets [20,27,28]. They have well-defined graphene lattices with many functional groups attached at the edges, which distinguishes them from carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), and carbon nanodots (CNDs) and gives them distinct quantum confinement and edge effect property [29–31]. Therefore, it is very suitable as a cocatalyst to construct C3N4-based heterogeneous catalysts.

To aid in designing and improving the bonding interface of the heterostructures, GQDs were synthesized utilizing a top-down method [32–35]. GQDs prepared using this method have uniform size, high purity, and strictly controlled oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface [36]. Contrarily, the bottom-up approach has unreliable synthesis routes, low purity, and challenges getting rid of organic small molecules because small molecules or polymers are used as the starting materials to create GQDs by dehydration, assembly, or carbonization [37].

In this work, o-GQDs with uniform size and enriched specific oxygen-containing groups were produced by cutting GO (graphene oxide) using nitric acid oxidation. The o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructure catalyst was constructed by a simple one-step hydrothermal procedure. It is worth mentioning that C3N4 was simply prepared by pyrolysis of melamine without any further modifications. The morphology, structure, and optoelectronic properties of the prepared samples were characterized using several physicochemical techniques. Unlike GO, we discovered and clarified the perfect bound heterostructure of o-GQDs with C3N4. According to our findings, the o-GQDs/C3N4 exhibit much better visible photocatalytic performance than those of C3N4, H-C3N4, and GO/C3N4.

Graphene oxide (GO), concentrated nitric acid HNO3 (68 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), melamine, and methyl orange (MO). All chemicals were analytical grade. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of greater than 18.5 MΩ cm−1 was used for the investigations.

Initial C3N4 was prepared by pyrolysis of melamine in a muffle furnace. Briefly, 10 g of melamine was heated from room temperature to 550 °C in a muffle furnace for 250 min and then held at 550 °C for 4 h to obtain bright yellow bulk C3N4, which was subsequently ground into a uniform powder. It was labeled as C3N4, and the prepared C3N4 did not need further modification.

The o-GQDs were prepared by refluxing GO in concentrated HNO3. In brief, 0.5 g GO and 250 mL of concentrated HNO3 were mixed at 140 °C and refluxed for 72 h in a round-bottom flask. The color of the solution turned from black to transparent yellow. Most of the HNO3 was recovered by a rotary evaporator and neutralized the remaining HNO3 with NaOH in an ice bath, then the solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 500 Da) for 5 days to remove salt impurities. Finally, the o-GQDs enriched with specific oxygen-containing groups were obtained by freeze-drying.

10 mg of o-GQDs and 1 g of C3N4 were mixed in 50 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 30 min to obtain a homogeneous mixture. To create the o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructure, the mixture was centrifuged and dried after being sealed in an autoclave for 6 h at 180 °C. Pure C3N4 was hydrothermally treated and labeled as H-C3N4. C3N4 and GO were hydrothermally treated and labeled as GO/C3N4 for comparative purposes.

The photocatalytic performance of the C3N4, H-C3N4, GO/C3N4, and o-GQDs/C3N4 was evaluated using MO as the contaminant. For each test, 100 mg of catalyst was dispersed in 100 mL of MO (10 mg/mL) in a photochemical reactor. A 300 W xenon lamp (with a protective case and cooling fan) was used as the light source with a filter (λ > 400 nm) to provide visible light. The dispersions were stirred in the dark for 30 min to reach adsorption and desorption equilibrium before light irradiation. After 5 mL of the sample were extracted and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min during the light reaction, the absorbance at wavelength 463 nm of the supernatant was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer.

The microscopic morphology and structure of GO, o-GQDs, and catalysts were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2-TF30, S-Twin) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Nova Namo-450, FEI). The phases of the GO, o-GQDs, and catalyst were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, RINT, Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation. The surface groups, valence, and chemical composition of the GO, o-GQDs, and catalysts were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet is 10), Raman spectroscopy (Horiba scientific Lab RAM HR,) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha+, Thermo fisher scientific). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area was estimated using ASAP2020HD88. The optical properties of GO, o-GQDs, and catalysts were investigated by UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600 plus, Japan) and fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000). The electrochemical performance of catalysts was evaluated by the electrochemical workstation. The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were detected using a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer.

The microstructure of the prepared o-GQDs enriched with specific oxygen-containing groups by oxidative cleavage of GO in concentrated HNO3 solution are shown in Fig. 1a and b. After continuous oxidative cutting, micron-scale GO was finally cut into nano-scale o-GQDs. The o-GQDs are monodisperse state and have uniform size distribution, with average particle size is ~3.68 nm (see Fig. 1b inset). The HR-TEM image of the prepared o-GQDs reveals that they exhibit high crystallinity and a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm in accordance with the typical (100) diffraction plane of graphene (see Fig. 1c). This result asserts that it is feasible to synthesize o-GQDs with great crystallinity, good dispersion, and uniform size. These particular properties facilitate the synthesis of o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures with an excellent distribution of o-GQDs on the surface.

Fig. 1d displays the XRD patterns of GO and o-GQDs. The typical diffraction peak of GO is a sharp peak at 2θ= 10.14°, which could be attributed to the diffraction of the graphene (001) plane. Meanwhile, there is a broad peak with weak intensity at 2θ= 26.48°, indicating that the degree of crystallinity of GO is low. After cutting with nitric acid oxidation, the peak of 2θ= 10.14° for the o-GQDs is disappeared, leaving only a more noticeable characteristic peak at 2θ= 26.48°, which may be ascribed to the diffraction of the graphene (002) plane.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the o-GQDs exhibit two distinct absorption peaks at 266 nm and 347 nm (see Fig. 1e), with the former corresponding to the π-π * transition of aromatic sp2 (CC), and the latter to the n-π * transition in sp3 (CO). The "Tyndall Effect" demonstrates the existence of colloidal particles (o-GQDs), which produce green light when exposed to 365 nm UV lamp radiation, and the inset displays the good water dispersion of o-GQDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra further show that o-GQDs has a band gap of 2.786 eV. The fluorescence spectra (see Fig. 1f) show that stable o-GQDs emission wavelengths (Em=517 nm) can be achieved over a range of excitation wavelengths (Ex =410–460 nm), with 460 nm serving as the ideal excitation wavelength. This excitation independence proves that the o-GQDs we have created are extremely pure, homogeneous in size, and have high dispersion [38].

The surface groups, valence states, and elemental makeup of the o-GQDs were examined using FTIR, Raman, and XPS characterization techniques. As seen in Fig. 2a, the FTIR spectra of o-GQDs can be easily differentiated from those of pure GO. The stretching vibrations of -OH, CO, CC, and C-O-C are represented by the peaks at 3400 cm−1, 1717 cm−1, 1600 cm−1, and 1062 cm−1, respectively. The CO, CC, and C-O-C peaks in the spectra of the o-GQDs are much more intense than those of the GO while the strength of the -OH peak is reduced. The stretching vibration of C-H is also represented by new peaks in the absorption of o-GQDs at 2700–3000 cm−1, and the in-plane bending vibration and out–of–plane bending vibration of C-H are represented by new peaks at 1407 cm−1 and 879 cm−1, respectively. According to FTIR spectra, the o-GQDs had more oxygen-containing groups than GO, including carboxyl and carbonyl groups.

Fig. 2b shows the Raman spectra of the GO and o-GQDs. The G and D peaks, which are situated at around 1585 cm−1 and 1374 cm−1, respectively, represent the in-plane stretching vibrations of C-atom sp2 hybridization (degree of graphitization) and, correspondingly, the disordered structure caused by flaws on the sp2-hybridized carbon hexagonal sheet. The intensity ratio of the D and G peaks (ID/IG), indicates the density of defects and the degree of oxidation in graphite-based materials. It is further evidence that the surface of the o-GQDs has more defects and oxygen-containing groups since the difference between the ID/IG values of the GO and the o-GQDs in this case is 0.958 against 1.034.

By using XPS analysis (see Fig. 2c), it is possible to learn more about the surface groups and chemical composition of the o-GQDs. The two primary peaks of the XPS survey spectra are found at 284.8 eV and 532.3 eV, respectively. These two peaks are characteristic for C1s and O1s, and the relative oxygen content of o-GQDs rises from 27 % to 38 % compared to that of the original GO. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s is shown in Fig. 2d, with three peaks at energies corresponding to CC/C-C, C-OH (hydroxyl), and CO (carbonyl) at 284.8 eV, 286.7 eV, and 288.2 eV, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of O1s are shown in Fig. 2e, with three peaks for carbonyl, carboxylic acid, and oxygen at 531.4 eV, 532.6 eV, and 533.5 eV, respectively. The surface oxygen-containing groups of the o-GQDs are significantly higher than those of the GO. According to high-resolution spectra of C1s and O1s, the hydroxyl groups (C-OH) are successfully converted into carbonyl (CO) and carboxyl groups(OC-O) as a result of the strong oxidation of the concentrated nitric acid. The results of the XPS analysis agree with the FTIR and Raman spectra.

The Zeta potentials of GO and o-GQDs are shown in Fig. 2f. The Zeta potential value of GO is − 34.6 eV, which indicates that its dispersion stability is poor. As evidenced by their Zeta potential value of − 90.5 eV, o-GQDs exhibit exceptional dispersion stability due to their extremely small nanoscale size and the concentration of oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface. These favorable conditions enable the subsequent formation of stable interfacial bonding and the uniform dispersion of o-GQDs with carbon nitride.

Uniform-sized GQDs have been created using a variety of high-temperature hydrothermal/solvothermal techniques. At temperatures above 150 °C, however, oxygen-containing groups on the surface of GQDs (such as carbonyl, carboxyl, or epoxide groups) would suffer severe decomposition [39,40]. Additionally, the traditional chemical oxidation approach for creating GQDs often calls for the use of potent oxidants such as concentrated sulfuric acid and potassium permanganate, both of which unavoidably include metal salt impurities and are laborious to handle afterward. We use pure concentrated nitric acid as a potent oxidant for metal-free reflux because GO may be completely transformed to o-GQDs without leaving any trace.

The o-GQDs preparation process might be broadly described as follows: The original GO surface is primarily made up of hydroxyl groups with a small amount of carboxyl and carbonyl groups; first, concentrated nitric acid attacks the initial defects of GO to produce additional defective sites (carbonyl and carboxyl groups), and the additional defective sites make GO easier to be attacked by concentrated nitric acid; Second, during the high-temperature oxidation of concentrated nitric acid, the bulk of the hydroxyl groups are converted into carbonyl and carboxyl groups; Finally, GO is fully separated into tiny graphene pieces (o-GQDs).

It is noteworthy that the o-GQDs we prepared are small and uniform in size, high in purity, and rich in oxygen-containing groups (carbonyl and carboxyl groups) on the surface. Numerous oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface give the o-GQDs superior solubility and dispersion, as well as greater electron jump and effective photocatalytic activity. By reducing the energy difference between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), the enhanced oxidation (CO) encourages the o-GQDs to absorb visible areas [41]. These many carbonyl and carboxyl groups provide the o-GQDs with strong photocatalytic activity.

More significantly, the o-GQDs' surface is rich in oxygen-containing groups including carboxyl, carbonyl, and other epoxy groups, which enables them to create strong interfacial connections with other materials without additional processing [29]. In particular, a strong chemical interaction may be created between the numerous carbonyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of the o-GQDs and the amino groups on the surface of C3N4. As a result, when the o-GQDs are hydrothermally treated with the C3N4, we are able to create a perfect combination of the o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures with high-efficiency photocatalytic activity.

The evolution of C3N4 to o-GQDs/C3N4 microscopic morphology was observed in detail using SEM. The initial C3N4 prepared by melamine pyrolysis is lamellar ( Fig. 3a). Interestingly, H-C3N4(Fig. 3b) obtained by hydrothermal treatment of C3N4, has the form of a nanorod-like structure. The GO/C3N4 and o-GQDs/C3N4 obtained by hydrothermal treatment of C3N4 with GO and o-GQDs, respectively, showed similar nanorod-like structure. This nanorod-like structure may significantly enhance the surface area of the catalyst, which may also dramatically expose the active sites and speed up mass movement in photocatalytic processes [42].

The development of o-GQD/C3N4 heterostructures with the o-GQDs of certain oxygen-containing groups will be more advantageous since these finer nanorod-like C3N4 will have more binding sites. Direct SEM examination of the o-GQD loading on the surface of C3N4 was not possible due to the small size of the o-GQDs. The combination of C3N4 and o-GQDs was thoroughly examined using TEM. In contrast, we were unable to detect any GO loading on the surface of the C3N4 in the TEM image of GO/C3N4 (Fig. 3f), indicating that it is challenging to combine GO with C3N4 by hydrothermal treatment. The original C3N4 displays a two-dimensional laminar structure (Fig. 3e).

Excitingly, hydrothermally treating the obtained o-GQDs with C3N4 allows for the appropriate formation of o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures. It is evident that the o-GQDs nanoparticles are equally distributed over the C3N4 surface (Fig. 3g). These nanoparticles have an average size of ~3.5 nm, and the HR-TEM picture also reveals that their lattice spacing is ~0.21 nm (Fig. 3h). This strongly suggests that the introduced o-GQDs are perfectly loaded on the surface of C3N4 and that the o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures is successfully constructed by hydrothermal treatment. The average size and lattice spacing of these particles are consistent with the prepared o-GQDs. This may be the main justification for enhancing the photocatalytic functionality of o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures.

The increased number of catalytically active sites and accelerated photocatalytic reactions are both aided by the greater specific surface area. The nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms of the catalysts are shown in Fig. 4a. The specific surface areas of the C3N4, H-C3N4, GO/C3N4, and o-GQDs/C3N4 are 7.137, 19.662, 14.523, and 20.006 m2/g, respectively. These values agreed well with the findings of the SEM investigation and the morphologies of the rod-like structures. The large size of GO covers the pores of C3N4 and decreases the specific surface area, resulting in a reduced specific surface area of GO/C3N4 compared to H-C3N4 due to the poor dispersion, ease of agglomeration of GO, and weak binding with the C3N4. It should be pointed out that the specific surface area of o-GQDs/C3N4 does not decrease compared to H-C3N4, which is due to their extremely small nanometer size and outstanding dispersion of the prepared o-GQDs. In comparison to C3N4, H-C3N4 and o-GQDs/C3N4 have more pores, and their pore sizes are mostly dispersed in the 5–30 nm range (Fig. 4b).

The different crystal structures of the catalysts were investigated by the XRD technique. The two characteristic diffraction peaks of C3N4 at 2θ= 27.2° and 12.7° ( Fig. 5a) correlate to the stacking of interlayers in the crystal's graphite-like structure in the first peak (002) and the in-plane structural packing motif of the tri-s-triazine units in the second peak (100), respectively. After hydrothermal treatment with o-GQDs, new peaks at 2θ= 6.1°, 10.5°, and 30.9° appear, indicating that the hydrothermal treatment enhances the crystallinity of the C3N4 [43].

Following the hydrothermal treatment of C3N4 with o-GQDs, the (002) peak position shifts from 2 = 27.2° to a large angle to 2 = 27.5° in Fig. 5b, indicating a shorter interlayer distance of o-GQDs/C3N4 due to an enhanced interlayer contact. Additionally, the o-GQDs/C3N4 diffraction peak intensity at (002) is a little bit lower than that of H-C3N4, which may be the cause of the o-GQDs' interference with the stacking of C3N4 [4,44]. This indirectly indicates that we achieved a trace loading of o-GQDs on the C3N4 surface.

Fig. 5c shows the FTIR spectra of the four catalysts. Peaks at 808 cm−1 correspond to the typical breathing pattern of the C3N4 triazine structure, whereas broad peaks at 3000–3600 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of N-H/O-H, and several strong absorption peaks in the 1200–1650 cm−1 region are associated with the stretching vibration of the CN heterocycle. Due to the low loading and uniform distribution of o-GQDs, the FITR spectra of C3N4, H-C3N4, GO/C3N4, and o-GQDs/C3N4 did not exhibit any discernible differences, suggesting that the hydrothermal treatment and creation of the o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures did not damage the conjugated CN structure of the C3N4.

XPS was used to analyze the chemical composition of the catalysts and the interaction between the o-GQDs and C3N4. Fig. 5d represents the high-resolution C1s spectra, with the peaks for CC/C-C, C-O, N-CN, and CO located at 284.8 eV, 286.6 eV, 288.1 eV, and 288.7 eV respectively. The increase in CO relative content in o-GQDs/C3N4 is attributed to the hydrothermal treatment as well as the carboxyl and carbonyl groups of the o-GQDs. In the XPS high-resolution spectra of N1s (Fig. 5e), the increase in the relative content of N-(C)3 in o-GQDs/C3N4 compare to C3N4 indicates a better crystallinity of the o-GQDs/C3N4. The binding energies of N-(C)2 and N-(C)3 of C3N4 correspond to 398.9 and 399.9 eV, respectively. However, for o-GQDs/C3N4, the binding energies of N-(C)2 and N-(C)3 are shifted to 398.5 and 399.6 eV, respectively. This peak shift phenomenon can be related to the interfacial contact of the two molecules, which suggests strongly that o-GQDs and C3N4 form an interfacial connection. Fig. 5f shows the high-resolution spectra of the O1s at 531.7 eV of C3N4, which corresponds to C-O, and a new peak for the o-GQDs/C3N4 at 533.5 eV, which corresponds to CO. The emerging CO of o-GQDs/C3N4 may be related to the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of o-GQDs. Owing mostly to hydrothermal processing and oxygen-containing groups on the surface of o-GQDs, o-GQDs/C3N4 display higher crystallinity and a higher oxygen content (carbonyl and carboxyl groups) than pure C3N4. Because of the superior interfacial bonding, homogeneous o-GQDs loading on the surface of C3N4, abundant oxygen-containing groups, and good crystallinity, the o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures show extraordinary photovoltaic properties and efficient photocatalytic activity.

The primary criteria for measuring the photocatalytic efficiency of o-GQDs/C3N4 was the degradation of MO in the presence of visible light. As shown in Fig. 6a, the blank experiments were performed without any catalyst addition, and it can be found that MO exhibits no self-degradation and is exceptionally stable in visible light. Similarly, the pristine C3N4 only adsorbs a trace amount of MO, and the degradation effect on MO can be negligible. In contrast, H-C3N4 and GO/C3N4 have similar degradation effects on MO, and the ratios of Ct/C0 (where the C0 and Ct were the UV–vis absorption intensity of MO at initial catalysis and each sampling.) are 0.74 and 0.69 with lower photocatalytic performance, respectively.

The o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures exhibit a remarkable MO degradation effect, with a ratio of Ct/C0 is 0.0092, achieving ~96.5 % degradation efficiency within 120 min and almost complete degradation (99.1 %) in 150 min. In comparison to the original C3N4, H-C3N4, and GO/C3N4, the photocatalytic efficiency of the o-GQDs/C3N4 is improved by 223 times, 18 times, and 14 times, respectively, according to the pseudo-first-order kinetics of ln (C0/Ct) = kt (see Fig. 6b). This finding clearly shows that the inclusion of o-GQDs can significantly improve the photocatalytic performance of the C3N4.

Stability and reusability are essential factors for o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures to improve in practical applications. By periodically collecting o-GQDs/C3N4 for MO degradation under the same conditions, we found that the o-GQDs/C3N4 catalyst still exhibits effective and acceptable degradation performance after five cycles of degradation (see Fig. 6c). Additionally, the crystal structure of o-GQDs/C3N4 did not significantly change even after several cycles of MO pollutants degrading (see Fig. 6d). This demonstrates the repeatability and stability of the C3N4/o-GQDs heterostructures.

To determine the mechanism underlying the increased catalytic activity of o-GQDs/C3N4, we looked at the optical and electrical properties of the four different prepared catalysts. According to Fig. 7a, which shows the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the samples, the absorption boundaries of all four catalysts are situated near 450 nm. It is possible that the creation of a heterostructure between o-GQDs and C3N4 can increase the utilization of visible light and the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs under visible light irradiation, significantly improving the efficiency of photocatalysis. The o-GQDs/C3N4 exhibits significant absorption throughout the visible region and can extend to the near-infrared region. C3N4 and H-C3N4 have almost no absorption in the visible region of> 450 nm.

The band gap energy (Eg) for C3N4, H-C3N4, GO/C3N4, and o-GQDs/C3N4 are 2.796, 2.868, 2.818, and 2.828 eV, respectively, according to the intercept of the targets to the Tauc plots (Fig. 7b). This shows that by slightly narrowing the band gap of H-C3N4, o-GQDs can enhance the absorption of visible light. Valence band X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (VB XPS) was used to characterize the valence band potentials of the samples in order to further study and assess the energy band structure of the catalysts and the photocatalytic activity. The results of VB XPS test (Fig. 7c and d) show that the valence band potentials for o-GQDs, C3N4, and o-GQDs/C3N4 are 2.723, 1.984, and 2.153 eV, respectively. In order to determine the energy band structures of o-GQDs and catalysts, the band gaps of o-GQDs, C3N4, and o-GQDs/C3N4 were previously determined to be 2.786, 2.796, and 2.828 eV, respectively (see Fig. 7e).

As indicated in Fig. 7e, the VB potential of C3N4 is 1.984 eV, while the valence band potential required to produce hydroxyl radicals(•OH) is 1.99 eV, indicating that C3N4 is difficult to generate •OH. This finding is consistent with the results of the subsequent ESR analysis. The VB potential of o-GQDs is 2.723 eV, which is significantly higher than the VB potential necessary to create •OH. Moreover, the hydrothermal treatment of o-GQDs and C3N4 yielded a 2.153 eV VB potential, showing that the o-GQDs/C3N4 has enough VB potential to generate •OH. It should be noted that even though the CB potential of C3N4 is much more negative than the CB potential required to generate •O
2
- (−0.33 eV), the fast photogenerated electron and hole recombination rate results in less •O
2
- being generated by C3N4. This also can be further verified in the subsequent ESR analysis. Due to the staggered energy band structure of type-II o-GQDs/C3N4, the electrons on the CB of o-GQDs rapidly transfer to the CB of C3N4, and the holes on the VB of C3N4 rapidly transfer to the o-GQDs, significantly boosting the migration of photogenerated electrons and holes.

The separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes can be evaluated by photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. With a decline in fluorescence emission intensity, electrons and holes separate more quickly and the photocatalyst's activity rises. As shown in Fig. 7f, the fluorescence intensity of the C3N4, H-C3N4, GO/C3N4, and o-GQDs/C3N4 all decreases gradually. In particular, the o-GQDs' ability to induce quick charge transfer and improve the separation efficiency of electrons and holes is demonstrated by the system's photocatalytic activity, which is supported by the significantly reduced fluorescence intensity.

Both the transient photocurrent response spectrum (Fig. 7g) and electrochemical impedance spectrum (Fig. 7h) were conducted in order to learn more about the catalysts' ability to perform photo-induced charge transfer. The photocurrent intensity of the o-GQDs/C3N4 is much higher than that of the C3N4, H-C3N4, and GO/C3N4 with the largest transient photocurrent response, which is three times higher in comparison to the pristine C3N4. This demonstrates that o-GQDs/C3N4 has stronger photoinduced charge separation and transfer efficiency as well as superior reactivity to visible light.

According to the electrochemical impedance spectrum, the o-GQDs/C3N4 Nyquist curve has the smallest arc radius, which shows that the material has a lower interfacial charge transfer resistance, facilitating quicker charge transfer for redox processes. Because they have a higher photo-induced charge transfer capacity, electrochemical studies demonstrate that the constructed o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures have the maximum efficiency for photocatalytic MO degradation. The test patterns of optical and electrical properties are consistent with the experimental results of MO catalyst degradation.

To identify the reactive species (RSs) in the photocatalytic degradation of MO by o-GQDs/C3N4, the ESR method and trapping experiments were used, as seen in Fig. 8a, b. The signals of •O
2
- and •OH of C3N4 and o-GQDs/C3N4 are undetected in the dark. For both C3N4 and o-GQDs/C3N4, the characteristic signal peaks of •O
2
- and •OH adducts are observed when exposed to visible light for 8 min. Particularly, the characteristic signal peaks for the •O
2
- and •OH adducts of o-GQDs/C3N4 are particularly considerably higher than those of C3N4 when exposed to visible light, suggesting that o-GQDs/C3N4 may produce more •O
2
- and •OH to take part in the photocatalytic activity. This is mainly attributed to the ability of o-GQDs to increase light absorption, adjust VB potential, and promote more photogenerated carriers to migrate to the catalyst surface to generate more •O
2
- and •OH through redox reactions with O2 and H2O. This is the primary cause for the efficient photocatalytic performance of the o-GQDs/C3N4. The extremely weak •OH and •O
2
- signal peaks of C3N4 could be attributed to the difficulty of generating •OH at the VB potential of C3N4 (1.984 eV) and the fast recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes.

For the RSs trapping experiments, we employed p-benzoquinone (PBQ), isopropanol (IPQ), and sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) as scavengers of superoxide radicals (•O
2
- ), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and photogenerated holes (h
+ ), respectively. As shown in Fig. 8c, d, the degradation efficiency of o-GQDs/C3N4 for MO is reduced to 23 %, 45 %, and 65 % after the addition of PBQ, IPQ, and Na2C2O4, respectively. This indicates that the major active component in the photocatalytic breakdown of MO is the superoxide radical •O
2
- , whereas the secondary active components are radical •OH and h
+ .

Fig. 9 illustrates the mechanism underlying the increased photocatalytic activity of the o-GQDs/C3N4. Due to a photo-response interval restricted to UV–vis light with a wavenumber of< 460 nm and fast recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, the initial C3N4 have poor photocatalytic activity under visible light [45]. By combining o-GQDs with certain oxygen-containing groups to form o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures, the many carbonyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of o-GQDs may be directly connected to the hydroxyl groups on the surface of C3N4 to generate a stable interfacial connection.

The π-conjugated bond between o-GQDs and C3N4 facilitates the interfacial contact and drives the carrier transfer from the CB space of C3N4 to the surface of o-GQDs [13], significantly increasing the separation rate of photogenerated electrons and holes and accelerates the charge transfer rate during the redox reaction [46]. In addition, it is feasible to greatly expand the photo-response range, enhance visible light usage, and effectively absorb and utilize visible and near-infrared light from sunlight by constructing o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures. This will benefit enhancing photocatalytic performance by increasing the photogenerated electron holes produced when exposed to visible light. o-GQDs/C3N4 may generate an abundance of active molecules with oxidative properties, such as •O
2
- , •OH, and h
+ , which oxidize and degrade organic pollutants into non-toxic water and carbon dioxide, through a series of redox reactions.

By one-step nitric acid oxidative cleavage of GO, the o-GQDs with uniform size and enriched particular oxygen-containing groups were effectively produced. The perfectly connected o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures metal-free catalyst was subsequently produced by a facile hydrothermal method.

The prepared o-GQDs/C3N4 heterostructures exhibited astonishing degradation efficiency for MO, which is infinitely higher than that of C3N4. The excellent photocatalytic performance of o-GQDs/C3N4 is a result of the perfect interfacial bonding between o-GQDs and C3N4, which results in high utilization of visible light and enhanced separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. In the end, o-GQDs/C3N4 produced more abundant amounts of •O2- and •OH and had the best photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the o-GQDs/C3N4 that we created has exceptional photocatalytic stability and reusability. Our proposed metal-free o-GQDs/C3N4 catalysts provide an efficient, environmentally friendly way to get rid of organic pollutants.

Youliang Nie: Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Rui Bao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Investigation. Jianhong Yi: Supervision. Jingmei Tao: Validation. Peng Liu: Methodology, Investigation. Ruofei Ma: Investigation. Haichuan Luo: Methodology. Danyang Ma: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Chinese National Science Foundation (Grant No. 52064032, 52174345), the Yunnan Science and Technology Projects Grants (Grant No. 202202AG050004, 202002AB080001).
